children.name: Alligator mississippiensis
children.description: Once threatened with extinction; recovered to over 5 million; keystone species creating gator holes — depressions that retain water through dry season and sustain entire wetland communities; an American conservation success story.

children.name: Alligator sinensis
children.description: Critically endangered; fewer than 120 wild individuals; confined to lower Yangtze River; heavily farmed.

children.name: Caiman crocodilus
children.description: Most abundant crocodilian; bony bridge between eyes gives spectacled appearance; millions harvested for leather; ecological keystone of Amazonian floodplains.

children.name: Caiman yacare
children.description: Dominant caiman of the Pantanal; estimated 10 million individuals; main prey of giant otters and jaguars.

children.name: Caiman latirostris
children.description: Distinguished by its exceptionally wide, rounded snout — an adaptation for crushing hard-shelled prey including freshwater snails and crabs. Found in marshes, mangroves and coastal lagoons of eastern South America from Brazil to Argentina. Severely depleted by hunting for its fine belly skin, but has recovered in parts of its range under legal protection and sustainable-use ranching programs. Unlike most caimans, females construct large mound nests from rotting vegetation and guard their eggs actively; juveniles produce high-pitched distress calls that trigger fierce maternal defence.

children.name: Melanosuchus niger
children.description: Largest predator in the Amazon basin at up to 5 m; hunts capybara, fish, and deer; nearly extinct by 1970s due to hunting; now recovering.

children.name: Paleosuchus trigonatus
children.description: Most terrestrial crocodilian; often found far from water in forest; bony armour protects from large predators.

children.name: Paleosuchus palpebrosus
children.description: The smallest living crocodilian, with adults rarely exceeding 1.5 m; named after the French naturalist Georges Cuvier who described it in 1807. Found throughout the Amazon and Orinoco river basins, favouring fast-flowing, cool forest streams where larger caimans rarely venture — an ecological niche apparently shaped by competition avoidance. Its extremely thick, heavily ossified skin, covering both belly and back, functions as armour against the anacondas and jaguars that share its habitat; the hide is too bony to be commercially useful, which inadvertently spared it from the mass hunting that decimated other caimans. Despite its small size, it is a capable predator of fish, amphibians and crustaceans.
